Technical Note · Power Cable Engineering

Short-Circuit Withstand of Insulated Cables

What the rating means, where the equations come from, how to apply them, and the mistakes that quietly undersize — or oversize — a cable.

Topic MV cable thermal limits Basis ICEA P-32-382 Format Engineering primer

A cable is sized first for the load it carries continuously. But the moment that governs whether it survives its lifetime is not the steady load — it is the fault. During a short circuit, current many times the rated value floods the conductor for a fraction of a second before protection clears it. In that brief window the conductor heats almost instantaneously, and if it gets hot enough it damages the insulation against it.

Short-circuit withstand is the cable’s ability to survive that thermal pulse without exceeding the temperature its insulation can tolerate. Getting it right is a safety and reliability requirement, not an optimization. Two questions fall out of it:

Verification — can an installed cable survive the fault current available at its location, for as long as the protection takes to clear it? And selection — what conductor size is needed to survive a given fault? Both are answered by the same small set of equations.

§1   First principleThe adiabatic assumption

The foundational simplification is that a fault is fast. Cleared in a few cycles up to a second or two, there is not enough time for meaningful heat to escape the conductor into the surrounding insulation. So all of the I²R heat generated during the fault is assumed to stay in the metal, raising its temperature. This is the adiabatic assumption — no heat loss during the fault.

It is the right assumption for short-circuit work because it is both simple and conservative: by ignoring the heat that genuinely does leak away, it slightly underestimates the cable’s true capability, erring safe. From an energy balance — heat generated equals heat stored in the metal’s mass — one arrives at the standard withstand relationship.

§2   The equationICEA P-32-382

For insulated conductors, the adiabatic withstand is expressed by ICEA P-32-382:

$$\left(\frac{I}{A}\right)^2 t = K \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T_2 + \lambda}{T_1 + \lambda}\right)$$

where $I$ is the RMS symmetrical fault current (A), $A$ the conductor cross-section (circular mils), $t$ the fault duration (s), and $T_1$, $T_2$ the initial and maximum-allowable conductor temperatures (°C). The constants embed each metal’s specific heat, density and resistivity, and the offset $\lambda$ is the inferred temperature of zero resistance:

Copper: $K = 0.0297$, $\lambda = 234$.    Aluminum: $K = 0.0125$, $\lambda = 228$.

A note on units: ICEA works in circular mils. One thousand circular mils is 1 kcmil; to convert from metric, multiply mm² by 1973.5. Standard AWG areas (4/0 = 211,600 cmil) are tabulated and worth keeping handy.

The three rearrangements you actually use

Allowable current for a known conductor and clearing time:

$$I = A\sqrt{\frac{K\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{T_2+\lambda}{T_1+\lambda}\right)}{t}}$$

Minimum conductor size for a known fault current and clearing time:

$$A = I\sqrt{\frac{t}{K\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{T_2+\lambda}{T_1+\lambda}\right)}}$$

Maximum clearing time a given conductor can tolerate at a given current:

$$t_{max} = \left(\frac{A}{I}\right)^2 K\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T_2+\lambda}{T_1+\lambda}\right)$$

§3   ShortcutThe short-circuit factor

Everything except $I$, $A$ and $t$ is fixed once you choose the metal and the two temperatures. Manufacturers pre-compute that block into a single short-circuit factor, $F_C$, reducing the equation to a form trivial to use:

$$I_{SC} = \frac{A \cdot F_C}{\sqrt{t}}, \qquad F_C = \sqrt{K\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T_2+\lambda}{T_1+\lambda}\right)}$$
Table 1 — Representative short-circuit factors $F_C$
Insulation (T₁ → T₂)CopperAluminum
Thermoset XLPE / EPR (90 → 250 °C)0.07190.0470
Thermoset XLPE / EPR (105 → 250 °C)0.06780.0443
Thermoplastic PVC / PE (75 → 150 °C)0.05290.0346

For these factors to be valid, the conductor must be assumed to start at its full continuous temperature $T_1$ when the fault strikes — the worst case, and the conservative one. If the cable actually runs cooler in service, its true withstand is higher.

§4   LimitsTemperature comes from the insulation

$T_1$ and $T_2$ are properties of the insulation system, which is what the conductor is protecting. The conductor itself could survive far hotter; the limit exists to keep the insulation intact.

Table 2 — Temperature limits by insulation
InsulationT₁ continuous (°C)T₂ short-circuit (°C)
XLPE / TR-XLPE90250
EPR90250
PVC (≤ ~300 mm²)75160
PVC (larger)75140
Paper / PILC80–85200–250

Thermoset insulations tolerate the highest short-circuit temperature (250 °C), which is why XLPE and EPR dominate medium-voltage cable. Thermoplastics soften at lower temperatures and carry correspondingly lower ceilings.

§5   WorkedThree quick examples

Allowable current. A 4/0 AWG aluminum, TR-XLPE cable (211,600 cmil; 90→250 °C; $F_C = 0.047$), with protection clearing in 30 cycles (0.5 s):

$$I_{SC} = \frac{211{,}600 \times 0.047}{\sqrt{0.5}} \approx 14{,}100 \text{ A} = 14.1 \text{ kA}.$$

Minimum size. A copper, thermoset feeder must survive 30 kA for 0.5 s:

$$A = \frac{30{,}000 \times \sqrt{0.5}}{0.0719} \approx 295{,}000 \text{ cmil} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{300 kcmil.}$$

And a 500 kcmil copper thermoset cable at 0.5 s withstands

$$\frac{500{,}000 \times 0.0719}{\sqrt{0.5}} \approx 50{,}800 \text{ A} = 50.8 \text{ kA.}$$

§6   TimeThe inverse-square-root law

The single most important behavioral feature of the withstand equation is its dependence on clearing time: withstand scales as $1/\sqrt{t}$. Halve the clearing time and the cable tolerates $\sqrt{2}\approx 1.41$ times more current; quadruple it and the tolerance halves.

This is why the design check is performed at the actual clearing time of the protection at that location — not the tabulated 30 cycles.

§7   The checkWithstand vs. available fault

The withstand equation gives only one side of the comparison. A real verification pairs it with the available fault current from a system short-circuit study, at the actual total clearing time of the upstream protection:

The verification

Withstand at $t$  ≥  available fault current,

where $t$ is the total clearing time — relay operating time plus breaker interrupting time, or fuse total-clear.

Two inputs come from outside the cable datasheet and are easy to get wrong. The available fault current comes from the system study — source impedance, transformer, the cable’s own impedance — not from a cable property. And the clearing time is the total time to interrupt; the 30 cycles printed in datasheets is a tabulation convention, not your project’s number. Because of the $1/\sqrt{t}$ law, using the right $t$ is as important as using the right fault current.

§8   RefinementAsymmetry

The withstand equation is written in terms of RMS symmetrical current. A real fault, especially close to a source with high X/R ratio, carries a decaying DC offset in the first cycles that raises the actual $\int i^2 dt$ above the symmetrical value. The cleanest practice is to keep the withstand on its symmetrical basis and inflate the fault side by an asymmetry factor appropriate to the local X/R and clearing time. At longer clearing times the DC has decayed and the effect is minor; at a few cycles with high X/R it is material and should not be neglected.

§9   Easily missedShields and neutrals

On shielded and concentric-neutral cable, the phase conductor is not the only metal that must survive a fault. The metallic shield or concentric neutral carries ground-fault return current, and it has its own short-circuit withstand — usually governed by a separate standard (ICEA P-45-482 for shields and sheaths; ICEA S-94-649 for concentric-neutral construction) and a lower temperature ceiling, because a bare neutral sits against the jacket and would damage it before reaching 250 °C.

The result is that such a cable has two independent short-circuit limits: the phase conductor, checked against the phase fault; and the shield or neutral, checked against the ground-fault current that returns through it. These answer to different fault currents, so the correct procedure is not “take the lower number” — it is to verify each limit against its own duty. The neutral check is frequently the binding one on solidly grounded systems, where ground-fault current is large, and comfortable on impedance- or resistance-grounded systems, where it is small. And only the portion of ground-fault current that actually returns on a given neutral counts — the rest divides among parallel neutrals, ground rods and earth, a current-split problem shared with substation grounding analysis.

§10   ContextThe standards landscape

Table 3 — Governing documents
StandardScope
ICEA P-32-382Short-circuit withstand of insulated conductors (the equations here)
ICEA P-45-482Short-circuit performance of metallic shields and sheaths
ICEA S-94-649Concentric-neutral cable construction, 5–46 kV
IEEE 242 (Buff Book)Protection and coordination; cable damage curves in practice
IEC 60949International equivalent, with an explicit heat-loss (non-adiabatic) correction

North American utility and industrial practice is built around the ICEA / IEEE family; international and IEC-specified projects use IEC 60949. They share the same adiabatic core, so results are close, but the conventions differ. The governing standard should be taken from the project specification, not assumed.

§11   CaveatWhen the adiabatic model breaks down

The adiabatic model is accurate and slightly conservative for fast faults — a few cycles up to roughly one to two seconds. Beyond that, real heat loss into the insulation becomes significant, and the equation increasingly underestimates the cable’s true capability, penalizing the design. For longer durations, IEC 60949 adds a correction (often written with an $\varepsilon$ term) that accounts for the escaping heat, yielding a higher and more realistic withstand. If a long clearing time is forcing an uneconomically large conductor, that correction is the tool to recover margin — provided the governing standard permits it.

§12   In practiceA design checklist

  1. Identify the metal and insulation → select $K$, $\lambda$, $T_1$, $T_2$ (or the published $F_C$).
  2. Get the available fault current from a system short-circuit study (symmetrical RMS).
  3. Apply asymmetry for the local X/R and clearing time, on the fault side.
  4. Get the actual total clearing time — relay plus breaker, or fuse total-clear — not the tabulated 30 cycles.
  5. Compute or look up the withstand at that clearing time.
  6. Compare: withstand ≥ asymmetric available fault. Document the margin.
  7. On shielded / concentric-neutral cable, repeat for the shield or neutral against the ground-fault return current at the ground-fault clearing time.
  8. For long clearing times, consider the IEC 60949 non-adiabatic correction if the standard allows.

§13   CloseConclusion

Cable short-circuit withstand reduces to one compact adiabatic equation, but applying it well is a matter of supplying the right inputs: the correct temperatures from the insulation, the correct fault current from a system study, the correct total clearing time from the protection, and — on shielded cable — a second check on the metallic return path against the ground fault. The equation is simple; the discipline is in not letting a single tabulated number stand in for a careful, two-sided comparison.

Notes on assumptions. All currents are RMS symmetrical unless stated. Withstand values follow the adiabatic ICEA P-32-382 form; short-circuit factors are representative for the stated metal / insulation / temperature combinations and should be confirmed against the specific product and governing standard. The adiabatic model is conservative for fast faults and grows more conservative with duration, where IEC 60949’s correction applies. Worked figures are illustrative; the protective-device curve in Figure 3 is schematic and does not represent a specific relay or fuse.
